prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. My friend who's a published novelist wrote this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

**What Works:**
*   **Voice and Tone:** The voice is authentic, unpretentious, and easy to read. It captures the voice of a student well—relatable and slightly self-deprecating.

Because the author is a novelist, the reader expects a bit more internal monologue or sensory description regarding the actual feeling of failure.
*   **Show, Don't Tell:** The essay relies heavily on summary. For example, the phrase "I was really upset for a while" tells the reader how the narrator felt. A stronger essay would describe the *action* of being upset (e.g., avoiding Marcus in the halls, ripping up the tryout number, etc.).
*   **The Conclusion:** The final paragraph is a bit generic ("failure isn't the end... it's just information"). It feels like a standard moral tacked onto the end rather than an organic realization derived from the specific story of the debate team.

**Summary:**
This is a solid, mechanically perfect piece of writing. It fulfills the prompt competently and has a strong thematic core. However, it plays it safe. Given the author is a novelist, the essay lacks the visceral imagery or emotional depth one might expect from a professional fiction writer; it reads more like a competent, high-achieving student who stuck strictly to the assignment. To get a 10/10, it needs to take more risks with descriptive language and dive deeper into the emotional fallout of the event.

prompt:

I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

Here is a second opinion on the essay, grading it based on standard criteria for a personal narrative assignment (clarity, structure, insight, and voice).

### Grade: 7/10

This is a solid, safe, and competent essay. It ticks all the boxes for the assignment, but it lacks the "spark" or stylistic flair one might expect from a professional novelist.

### What Works
*   **Structure and Pacing:** The essay is well-organized. It moves logically from the setup (the driveway practice) to the conflict (tryouts), the climax (getting cut), and the resolution (joining debate). The pacing is brisk and keeps the reader engaged.
*   **Specific Dialogue:** The line *"just stay out of the way"* is the strongest moment in the piece. It’s a sharp, painful detail that conveys the reality of the situation more effectively than the surrounding descriptions.
*   **Clarity:** The writing is clean and easy to follow. There is no confusion about the timeline or the lesson learned, which makes it an easy read.

### What Doesn't Work
*   **Show, Don't Tell:** This is the biggest weakness. The essay summarizes emotions rather than immersing the reader in them.
    *   *Example:* "I was really upset for a while." → A novelist would usually show this through action (e.g., staring at the ceiling, ripping up the tryout sheet, avoiding Marcus's calls).
    *   *Example:* "The other kids were faster and taller." → Specific imagery (e.g., "They loomed over me like giants," or "I was gasping for air while they barely broke a sweat") would have made the failure feel more visceral.
*   **Generic Insight:** The lessons learned ("difference between fun and competition," "failure isn't the end") are true, but they are also very common conclusions for this type of essay. It feels a bit like a standard template response rather than a unique personal philosophy.
*   **Lack of Stylistic Flair:** Given the author is a published novelist, the prose is surprisingly plain. There is very little variation in sentence structure, and the vocabulary is functional rather than evocative. It reads more like a capable high school student fulfilling an assignment than a writer flexing their creative muscles.

### Summary
If this were written by a typical student, it would be a B+ essay—it does exactly what the teacher asked. However, knowing the author's background, it feels like an "off-day" or perhaps an intentional exercise in restraint. It is technically correct but emotionally flat.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. My friend who's a published novelist wrote this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. My friend who's a published novelist wrote this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. My friend who's a published novelist wrote this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

Here is my assessment and grading:

### Grade: **9/10**

### What Works
*   **Strong Voice and Tone:** The essay has a natural, conversational voice that draws the reader in immediately. It doesn’t feel overly academic or forced; it sounds like a real person reflecting on an honest experience.
*   **Excellent "Hook" and closer:** The opening establishes the stakes (driveway vs. reality) well, but the conclusion is the standout. The final sentence—*"failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information"*—is a sophisticated, mature insight that elevates the entire piece.
*   **Specificity:** Using specific terms like "pick and roll" and "fast break drill" grounds the narrative in reality. It shows rather than tells, allowing the reader to visualize the scene.
*   **The Narrative Arc:** The pivot to the debate team is a great structural choice. It prevents the essay from being just a "sad story" about basketball and turns it into a story about discovery and finding one's strengths.
*   **Nuance:** The realization regarding the Dunning-Kruger effect (though not named as such)—*"you don't know what you don't know"*—is a high-level insight for a short personal narrative.

### What Doesn’t Work (Areas for Improvement)
*   **Length/Depth:** The prompt asked for ~500 words, and this essay looks to be around 350–400 words. While concise writing is generally a virtue, in a school setting, this is "underdeveloped." The middle section—specifically the emotional fallout of the failure—is rushed. We are told "I was really upset," but the essay would benefit from slowing down to *show* that emotion rather than just stating it.

**Verdict:** It is an excellent, polished piece of writing that fulfills the prompt with style. It misses a perfect score only because it falls short of the length requirement and could use more sensory detail in the reflection phase.

prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. My friend who's a published novelist wrote this. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

### **Grade: 8.5/10**

This is a solid "A" paper. It is mechanically flawless, structurally sound, and answers the prompt with maturity. However, it plays it safe and misses the opportunity to be truly memorable.

### **What Works (The Professional Touch)**
*   **Efficiency of Prose:** Your novelist friend knows how to cut the fat. There isn’t a single wasted word here. The sentence structures are varied, and the flow is effortless. They avoid the common student trap of over-explaining or using flowery language to sound smart.
*   **Concrete Specificity:** The essay grounds the failure in specific details rather than vague feelings. The mention of the "pick and roll" and the "fast break drill" establishes the setting immediately. It shows, rather than tells, that the narrator was unprepared.
*   **The "Kicker" Quote:** The line *"failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information"* is a very strong, sophisticated takeaway. It reframes a cliché lesson into something sharp and insightful.
*   **Authentic Voice:** The voice is casual and conversational without being sloppy. Phrases like *"Turns out playing in your driveway..."* and *"So yeah, I failed..."* feel natural for a student narrator.

### **What Doesn’t Work (The Critique)**
*   **Emotional Distance:** The biggest weakness is that it feels a bit too polished. The narrator recounts the events with a sense of detachment. We are told the narrator was "really upset," but we don't *feel* the sting of that rejection or the specific shame of the "stay out of the way" comment. *   **The "Debate" Pivot:** The transition to the debate team is handled logically, but it happens very fast. It feels like a box being checked rather than a genuine exploration of how the failure changed the writer's internal life.

### **Verdict**
If a student turned this in, you would praise them for their clarity, grammar, and structure. You would likely encourage them to slow down during the emotional moments to let the reader feel the pain of the failure alongside them. The fact that a novelist wrote this makes sense—it is technically perfect, but it lacks the raw messiness you often see in genuine student writing.
